learning_outcomes.description: Critically analyse the challenges of leading and managing people in the workplace.
Critically analyse the application of various theoretical frameworks of leadership.
Develop and activate a plan to build upon your strengths and weaknesses in leading and managing people.
Clearly articulate the fundamental skills of leadership.
Practise effective leadership & business management skills in a variety of situations.

assessments.task_description:
This assessment is designed to guide you through a structured and reflective exploration of the values that shape the type of leader you aspire to be. Across three interconnected weekly tasks, you will move from imagining the kind of world you want to live in, to identifying the key themes that underpin that vision, and finally to uncovering the core values that drive your thinking and behaviour.

By engaging in this process consistently each week, you will build a deeper understanding of the principles that matter most to you and develop greater self-awareness—an essential foundation for authentic and effective leadership.

TASK 1 - RESPONSE (Week 3) - 5 marks - Word count: 150–200 words (anything over is not marked)

Instructions: Reflect deeply on the following question: “What is the type of world you want to live in? Your response should express your personal vision of an ideal world. This is not an abstract or hypothetical exercise, it is an opportunity to articulate the conditions, relationships, opportunities, and ways of living that you believe are important. Write honestly and thoughtfully about what matters to you and why.

Purpose of this task: To outline your hopes, beliefs, and aspirations about the world, which will form the foundation for identifying the themes and values that shape your leadership identity.

What to submit: A short written reflection (150–200 words) describing the world you want to live in – via Turnitin.

TASK 2 – THEMES (Week 4) - 5 marks

Word count: Maximum 300 words (anything over is not marked)

Instructions: Review your response from Task 1 and look for the deeper ideas that sit beneath the surface. These are the themes (or criteria) that shaped how you judged your desired world (e.g., Belonging, fairness, and personal accountability).

Ask yourself:

  • Which of these features or qualities were most important in the world I described?
  • What ideas or principles guided my thinking, even if I didn’t explicitly name them?
  • Which of these criteria did I implicitly use to define what my desired world would look like?

From this analysis:

  1. Identify three (3) clear themes/criteria that capture the essence of your response from Task 1.
  2. For each theme, write around 50 words explaining what the theme means and why it is essential to your desired world.

Purpose of this task:

To help you clarify the patterns and guiding principles that underpin your vision. These themes become the bridge between your imagined world and the values you will identify in Task 3.

What to submit:

Three (3) identified themes/criteria with short explanations (up to 300 words total) – via Turnitin

TASK 3 - VALUES (Week 5) - 5 marks

Word count: Maximum 150 words (anything over is not marked)

Instructions: Each theme or criterion from Task 2 is grounded in a deeper value or core belief that shapes how you understand and interpret the world (e.g., you may feel that the theme ‘fairness’ is grounded in the value of ‘respect’).

For each of your three (3) themes:

  • Identify one (1) value that underpins it (one or two words is enough).
  • Define or explain each value in around 50 words, showing how it connects directly to the theme it supports.

Purpose of this task:

To help you uncover the core values that guide your thinking, behaviour, and leadership approach. These values form the foundation of your emerging leadership identity.

What to submit:

Three (3) values (one per theme identified in Task 2), each accompanied by a brief definition (maximum 150 words total) – via Turnitin.

AI STATEMENT:

Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance.

A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You will submit your work via Turnitin (weekly submission during weeks 3, 4, and 5).

assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
This is an individual assessment.

The test will consist of (a) 50 Multi-choice questions, and (b) 3 short answer questions.

The test reflects the topics covered in the course from week 1 to week 7. Questions will be based on the topics covered in the textbook, UQx BUSLEAD modules, and seminars; so it will be vital for students to attend the seminars and complete their readings each week.

AI Statement:

This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: This is a paper-based assessment where students are required to handwrite their responses. It is essential that students ensure their handwriting is clear and legible. If handwriting is not legible, it may result in the responses being marked incorrectly or not being marked at all, which could negatively impact the student's overall grade. The allowed sheet of notes must be submitted with the exam. You will need to use a 2B pencil for the multiple choice section, and bring an eraser/rubber so you can easily change an answer if needed.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

assessments.task_description:
OVERVIEW

Assessment 3 moves you from reflection to action. It builds directly on Assessment 1 (Weeks 3–5), in which you identified a vision of the world you want to live in, distilled three themes/criteria, and defined three core values.

In Assessment 3, you will select two (2) of those values and embody them through new, real‑world leadership actions, then critically reflect on what you learned about yourself and leadership. This assessment develops your capacity for authentic and transformational leadership through practice, reflection, and dialogue. At the end of the semester, you will deliver a presentation reflecting on your experience in doing the Embodying Values Activities, and how the activities contribute to your Transformational and Authentic leadership capacity. You are also required to peer-review two other presentations.

Attendance in both Week 12 and Week 13 is mandatory as you will either be presenting or peer‑reviewing each week:

The class will be split into two groups (allocation will be posted on Blackboard):

  • Group 1: Presents Week 12, peer‑reviews Week 13
  • Group 2: Peer‑reviews Week 12, presents Week 13

TASK REQUIREMENT – 3 PARTS

PART A – Embody Two Values (Weeks 6-11)

Select two (2) values from Assessment 1 Task 3 and design two Embodying Values Activities (one activity per value). Each activity must:

  • Be new to you (not something you have done before in any form; not routine study/work/volunteering/personal habits).
  • Be intentionally planned and commenced after completing Assessment 1 (Weeks 3–5).
  • Take you beyond your usual repertoire and comfort zone.
  • Provide clear benefit to another person or group.
  • Involve active, embodied participation (not observation or donation alone).

Activity examples (not exhaustive):

Volunteering for the first time with a community organisation; taking on a new service/advocacy role in an unfamiliar context; initiating a leadership action in a setting where you have not previously held responsibility.

Important:

  • Retrospective activities are not permitted.
  • Donations alone do not count (nor are they required); if included they must be complemented by an embodied action.
  • If you’re uncertain about whether an activity is sufficiently new or aligns tightly with your value, check with the lecturer by the end of Week 6 so you can refine your plan.

PART B — Presentation (Week 12-13)

Effective leaders communicate ideas convincingly. Your task is to present a maximum 7‑minute reflection (recommend ≤10 slides) that:

  • Names the two values you selected (from Assessment 1) and why you chose them.
  • Describes the two new actions you undertook to embody each value.
  • Reflects critically on challenges, tensions, and learning (not just description).
  • Analyses how your experience developed your transformational and/or authentic leadership.
  • Integrates at least five (5) academic journal articles; use APA 7th ed. and cite on slides.
  • Ends with a clear conclusion that synthesises key insights.
  • Is presented in a professional, engaging and clear manner.

This is not a research presentation, but your reflection must demonstrate engagement with leadership literature by drawing on relevant theories, concepts, and language.

Submission: Upload your slides (PPT or equivalent, not PDF) via Turnitin. Presentations are recorded for marking purposes as per UQ policy.

PART C — Peer Review (Weeks 12–13)

You will assess two (2) other presentations assigned to you in Blackboard using the provided Proforma (approx. 150 words ±10% each). Offer specific, constructive, respectful feedback grounded in leadership concepts.

Why peer-review?

The skill to provide honest and constructive feedback on others' performance is a key skill for leaders. Engaging in the peer-review task helps you cultivate critical analytical skills and the ability to articulate constructive feedback, which is indispensable for effective leadership. Through this process, you not only enhance your evaluative capabilities but also refine your communication skills, laying the groundwork for becoming adept leaders capable of fostering growth and improvement in collaborative environments.

What is required?

  • Use the PROFORMA document that is available on Blackboard (in the Assessment 3 folder) to complete your peer review.
  • Offer specific, constructive, respectful feedback grounded in leadership concepts.
  • Submit your peer review document via Blackboard.
  • Failure to attend the presentation on the due date will incur a penalty.

Note: If you are unable to conduct a peer review for either of the two presenters due to the peers’ failure to submit their presentation materials, please note in your peer review document: “The presentation materials were not submitted within the designated timeframe.

ACADEMIC INTEGRITY & AI/MT USE

This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

learning_activities.topic:
Welcome & Introduction

This seminar provides an overview the course content, expectations and assessment items. Additionally, we explore the difference between management and leadership, and discuss what it takes to be a good leader.
learning_activities.topic_description: Welcome & Introduction This seminar provides an overview the course content, expectations and assessment items. Additionally, we explore the difference between management and leadership, and discuss what it takes to be a good leader.

learning_activities.topic:
Theories evolution + The Trait Approach

This seminar explores the evolution of leadership theories throughout the 20th century. Additionally, we will examine the classic Trait approach to leadership.
learning_activities.topic_description: Theories evolution + The Trait Approach This seminar explores the evolution of leadership theories throughout the 20th century. Additionally, we will examine the classic Trait approach to leadership.

learning_activities.topic:
The Skills Approach to Leadership

This seminar explores another leader-centered approach: the Skills Approach. This approach highlights that what sets a leader apart from their peers are the leader's competencies and skills. Building on this perspective, researchers have developed models to explain the capabilities (knowledge and skills) that enable effective leadership.
learning_activities.topic_description: The Skills Approach to Leadership This seminar explores another leader-centered approach: the Skills Approach. This approach highlights that what sets a leader apart from their peers are the leader's competencies and skills. Building on this perspective, researchers have developed models to explain the capabilities (knowledge and skills) that enable effective leadership.

learning_activities.topic:
The Behavioural Approach to Leadership

This seminar covers the Behavioural Approach to Leadership. This approach looks at leadership as an observable activity or process. Following this perspective, researchers in the 1940s began to identify the behaviours that were associated with effective leadership.
learning_activities.topic_description: The Behavioural Approach to Leadership This seminar covers the Behavioural Approach to Leadership. This approach looks at leadership as an observable activity or process. Following this perspective, researchers in the 1940s began to identify the behaviours that were associated with effective leadership.

learning_activities.topic:
The Situational Approach to Leadership

This seminar explores an approach that, unlike previous ones, is not leader-centric: the Situational Approach. This approach focuses on leadership in various situations, suggesting that different circumstances require different types of leadership. According to this approach, an effective leader is one who can adapt their leadership style to suit different situations. This approach is among the first to consider the role of followers in determining a leader's effectiveness.
learning_activities.topic_description: The Situational Approach to Leadership This seminar explores an approach that, unlike previous ones, is not leader-centric: the Situational Approach. This approach focuses on leadership in various situations, suggesting that different circumstances require different types of leadership. According to this approach, an effective leader is one who can adapt their leadership style to suit different situations. This approach is among the first to consider the role of followers in determining a leader's effectiveness.

learning_activities.topic:
Leader-Member Exchange (LMX) Theory

This seminar explores Leader-Member Exchange Theory (LMX). LMX is based on the assumption that the relationship between the leader and followers is variable; that is some of the followers may receive more attention from the leader than other followers. These followers are referred to as the 'in-group'. Other followers, the 'out-group', receive less of the leader's attention and time. In this seminar, we will learn about the advantages of being an in-group member, and how one can become an in-group member.

Good Friday Public Holiday - Friday 3rd April 2026 - Check Blackboard for announcements about affected classes.

These followers are referred to as the 'in-group'. Other followers, the 'out-group', receive less of the leader's attention and time. In this seminar, we will learn about the advantages of being an in-group member, and how one can become an in-group member. learning_activities.topic:
Team Leadership

This seminar explores team leadership and what a group member (not the team leader) can do to provide leadership when necessary and without having the position power. 

Centrally run In-Semester Examination
learning_activities.topic_description: Team Leadership This seminar explores team leadership and what a group member (not the team leader) can do to provide leadership when necessary and without having the position power. Centrally run In-Semester Examination

learning_activities.topic:
Revision and Exam

See Assessment 2 information for details of the exam
learning_activities.topic_description: Revision and Exam See Assessment 2 information for details of the exam

learning_activities.topic:
Transformational Leadership

This seminar explores Transformational Leadership, one of the most current and popular approaches. You will learn about five qualities of transformational leaders and examine examples to better understand Transformational Leadership.
learning_activities.topic_description: Transformational Leadership This seminar explores Transformational Leadership, one of the most current and popular approaches. You will learn about five qualities of transformational leaders and examine examples to better understand Transformational Leadership.

learning_activities.topic:
Authentic Leadership

This seminar explores the topic of Authentic Leadership. Authentic leadership is concerned with a simple yet very important question: 'what is good leadership?' Authentic Leadership is very similar to Transformational Leadership, with more explicit emphasis on the role of ethics in leadership.
learning_activities.topic_description: Authentic Leadership This seminar explores the topic of Authentic Leadership. Authentic leadership is concerned with a simple yet very important question: 'what is good leadership?' Authentic Leadership is very similar to Transformational Leadership, with more explicit emphasis on the role of ethics in leadership.

learning_activities.topic:
Leadership and Organisational Culture

This seminar explores organizational culture and its connection to leadership. We will see how leaders shape and are shaped by the culture of their organization, and how a strong culture can improve leadership.
learning_activities.topic_description: Leadership and Organisational Culture This seminar explores organizational culture and its connection to leadership. We will see how leaders shape and are shaped by the culture of their organization, and how a strong culture can improve leadership.
